emitted.title: Force-unwrap on network response that can be nil
emitted.body: Line 15: `return response!` force-unwraps the result of `network.fetchUser(id:id)`, which previously returned `User?`. If the network layer returns `nil` for a valid reason (user not found, cache miss, etc.), this will crash at runtime.
emitted.suggested_action: Remove the force-unwrap and return `response` directly, or handle the nil case explicitly with `guard` or `??`.

expected.rationale: response! crashes if network returns nil; should propagate as throws or return Optional

emitted.title: Force-unwrap on UserDefaults value that may not exist
emitted.body: Line 19: `UserDefaults.standard.string(forKey: "username")!` will crash if the "username" key has never been set or was removed. UserDefaults.string(forKey:) returns an optional.
emitted.suggested_action: Return an optional `String?` or provide a default value using `?? "DefaultName"`.

expected.rationale: UserDefaults.string returns Optional<String>; force-unwrapping crashes on first launch when key is absent

emitted.title: Source-breaking return type narrowing
emitted.body: The return type changed from `User?` to `User`, removing optionality. This is a source-breaking change that will break existing callers who use optional binding (`if let user = try await fetchUser(...)`) or optional chaining. Callers that depend on the optional return type will fail to compile.
emitted.suggested_action: Revert to `User?` return type or introduce this as a new method (e.g., `fetchRequiredUser`) while deprecating the old signature to provide a migration path.

expected.rationale: Public method changed from User? to User — source-breaking change for callers. Per steering, source-breaking signature changes are blocker.

emitted.title: Non-optional return type implies guaranteed value
emitted.body: The public method returns a non-optional `String`, but the implementation force-unwraps a value from UserDefaults that may be nil. The API signature promises a `String` will always be returned, creating a misleading contract. Callers have no indication that this method can fail if the key is missing.
emitted.suggested_action: Change the return type to `String?` to accurately reflect that the username may not be present.

expected.rationale: currentUserName() returns non-optional String but the underlying UserDefaults.string(forKey:) is Optional<String>. The signature lies to callers about whether nil is possible. Should be -> String? or take a default.

emitted.title: New public method `currentUserName()` has no test coverage
emitted.body: A new public method `currentUserName()` was added without any corresponding test in this diff. This method reads from UserDefaults and force-unwraps the result, making it critical to verify its behavior with tests covering both the success case (when the key exists) and edge cases (when the key is missing or nil).
emitted.suggested_action: Add test cases for `currentUserName()` covering successful retrieval and the nil/missing key scenario.

expected.rationale: currentUserName() is a new public method introduced in this diff with no accompanying test. Per test_coverage.md, new public/open declarations without a test entry point in the diff are flagged at severity major.

emitted.title: Missing [weak self] causes retain cycle in Combine sink
emitted.body: The `.sink { event in self.handleEvent(event) }` closure captures `self` strongly. Because the sink is stored in `self.cancellables`, this creates a retain cycle: `self` → `cancellables` → `sink` → `self`. The subscription will never be released, causing a memory leak.
emitted.suggested_action: Change line 17 to `.sink { [weak self] event in` and unwrap `self` inside the closure.

expected.rationale: The .sink closure captures self strongly and is stored in cancellables, which is owned by self — a textbook Combine retain cycle. Per correctness.md blocker definition (`will crash, corrupt data, or violate a documented invariant`), a retain cycle leaks memory but does not crash; therefore severity is major, not blocker.
